entries.intendedFor: Public — executives and companies building with AI
Public — executives and companies building with AI
Public — executives and companies building with AI
Public — executives and companies building with AI
Public / AInchors audience — Building in Public blog

findings: Named individual 'Angie' identified with private onboarding status. Exact text: 'Angie onboarding nudge — Day 3 of no contact. Aria to warm check-in on Saturday. Onboarding is still blocked at Stage 1 and the JotForm API key has been outstanding since 28 April.' (Section: What's Next). Combined with existing public context (Angie Foong, CEO), this individual is identifiable. Discloses: (a) name, (b) business relationship status, (c) contact frequency, (d) a specific technical failure (JotForm API key), (e) planned AI contact action — all without evident consent.
recommended_fixes: Remove 'Angie onboarding nudge' bullet entirely from public blog, OR anonymise: replace 'Angie' with 'a prospective client' and remove the JotForm API key detail and the specific date (28 April).

findings: Specific financial figures disclosed publicly: today's spend $59.60, confirmed balance $115.00, 7-day cumulative total $862.28, Day 3 spend $338.76, Day 6 spend $166.10, $85 discrepancy. These figures, in combination, reveal AInchors' daily operational burn rate, cash-in-hand balance at a specific date, and spending pattern over 7 days. This is a 'Building in Public' blog so some disclosure is intentional, but the account balance figure ($115.00 remaining after top-up) combined with burn rate is sensitive competitive intelligence.

Internal cron job ID disclosed: 'dce1ada4' (AKB daily update cron). Exact text: 'route the AKB daily update cron (dce1ada4, runs at 03:00 AEST)'. Cron IDs can assist an attacker in targeting specific scheduled processes.
Internal script names disclosed: obs-collector.sh, auto-heal.sh, cost-tracker.sh, cost-state.json, openclaw.json. These reveal attack surface detail.
Internal document name disclosed: YODA_OC1_OC2_OPERATIONAL_BRIEF.md — reveals internal naming conventions and system architecture.
State variable names disclosed: stuckSessions, spentSinceTopUp, bootstrapMaxChars, bootstrapTotalMaxChars — reveals internal configuration structure.
recommended_fixes: Remove the specific cron ID 'dce1ada4' — replace with 'the AKB daily update cron'.
Consider whether the $115 balance figure adds value vs risk. If keeping, note it's a one-time snapshot, not standing balance.
Internal script names are borderline — acceptable for a technical transparency blog but consider if they reveal more than intended.

findings: Anthropic billing claim: 'Anthropic's input_cache_write_5m fees don't appear in per-session token totals.' This is stated as a definitive fact. If Anthropic provides these in some dashboard views or API responses, this claim may be misleading or inaccurate. Could attract pushback from Anthropic.

OpenClaw CVE reference: Security audit table states 'OpenClaw >= v2026.1.29 (CVE patched)' without naming the CVE. Referring to an unnamed CVE in a public blog could cause unnecessary concern among OpenClaw users who can't verify what was patched.

Benchmark claims for named third-party models (qwen3.5:cloud latency figures, kimi-k2.6 / glm-5.1 / deepseek-v4 being subscription-gated) are dated point-in-time measurements. No caveat that these may change. Could be disputed by model providers.
recommended_fixes: Add qualifier to Anthropic billing claim: 'In our experience, ...' or 'At the time of writing, ...'
Either name the CVE or soften to 'security patch applied per vendor release notes'.
Add a disclaimer footnote on benchmark data: 'Results reflect testing on [date]; model performance and pricing subject to change.'

findings: All content appears to be original work by AInchors/Ken Mun. No third-party quotes, excerpts, or code snippets requiring attribution detected. Code references are to internal scripts.

Australian Privacy Act (Privacy Act 1988) — APP 6 (Use or disclosure of personal information): Publishing 'Angie's' onboarding status, contact history, and a specific technical failure (JotForm API key outstanding since 28 April) in a public blog post constitutes disclosure of personal information for a purpose beyond the primary purpose of collection (onboarding), without evident consent. Even first-name-only usage, combined with the company's public profile, makes this individual likely identifiable.

GDPR consideration (if EU readers access the public blog): Same individual identification risk applies under GDPR Article 6 (lawful basis) and Article 5(1)(b) (purpose limitation). Publishing client onboarding data without consent lacks a clear lawful basis.
recommended_fixes: Remove or fully anonymise the 'Angie' bullet in 'What's Next'. This is the primary regulatory risk and the fix is simple: remove or anonymise.

findings: Memory truncation revelation: 'The platform was starting each session with a truncated world model, producing wrong answers, and emitting no warning that anything was missing. No error. No alert. No indication. Just quietly wrong context, session after session.' — Transparent and honest, but prospective clients reading this may question the reliability of AInchors' AI systems. Framing mitigates this somewhat (it's fixed) but it's a reputational risk.

$85 accounting ghost: Revealing that the internal cost tracker was structurally broken and had been hiding $85 in charges over 6 days is candid but could raise questions about operational rigour from potential investors or clients.
The 'What's Next' bullet disclosing Angie's non-responsiveness ('Day 3 of no contact') could embarrass AInchors professionally if she sees it — implies she's being tracked and is unresponsive.
recommended_fixes: The memory truncation and accounting ghost stories are part of the 'building in public' ethos — acceptable. Consider adding a sentence on detection/remediation cadence to reinforce operational discipline.
Remove the Angie bullet (also addresses Privacy FAIL).

findings: Average latency calculation error. Blog states: 'Average latency across all six benchmarks: 58 seconds'. Actual average from the published table: (37.3 + 87.5 + 70.9 + 58.2 + 1.9 + 37.6) / 6 = 48.9 seconds. The stated figure of 58 seconds is wrong by ~9 seconds (~18% error). This is verifiable by any reader who checks the table. Publishing incorrect benchmark data in a technical blog undermines credibility.
line_refs: Section: The PoC That Revealed Its Own Limits > Benchmark Results
recommended_fixes: Correct '58 seconds' to '48.9 seconds' (or '~49 seconds' for readability).

must_fix_before_publish: FAIL-1: Remove or anonymise the 'Angie' onboarding bullet in 'What's Next' (Privacy/PII + Regulatory)
FAIL-7: Correct average latency from '58 seconds' to '48.9 seconds' (Accuracy)
FAIL-5: Angie disclosure also triggers regulatory concern (APP 6 / GDPR) — same fix as FAIL-1
should_fix: WARN-2a: Remove cron ID 'dce1ada4' from public post
WARN-2b: Consider removing or genericising internal document name YODA_OC1_OC2_OPERATIONAL_BRIEF.md
WARN-3a: Add qualifier to Anthropic billing claim
WARN-3b: Soften CVE reference or name the CVE
WARN-3c: Add benchmark data disclaimer (date, subject to change)
WARN-6: Angie bullet also a reputational risk (same fix as FAIL-1)
